title: I suck at this, what to do?
author: Argose83
selftext: Wondering if AI could help with my awful instincts. Don't know where to be gone with such a small amount though

comments.author: Low_Dress6063
comments.body: Why did you hold so long. Max loss per trade should be 1-3% of your balance.

replies.author: Argose83
replies.body: I was soa afraid of losing, when everyone said that it was about to boom, that I made myself lose way more. I like to think I've learned my lesson, all stop losses from now on.

replies.author: Low_Dress6063
replies.body:
Bro, your jumping in pump and dumps. Its like the pyramid scheme of the stock market.

Stick with a sp500 etf like spy, spym, voo.

Or if you want to make sure you don't lose money treasury notes anf ee bonds from the government are 4%-5% a year right now.

replies.author: Argose83
replies.body: Yea that def seems safer 😝 I've been looking at etfs for ai and space lately? But you would suggest whole sectors?

replies.author: Low_Dress6063
replies.body:
If your going to go with sector etfs you'll need to stay up to date in the latest news. Right now it's software and cyber security. BUG, HACK, IGV. However, your about 3 days late to the party.. 

Sectors rotate, jan-may it was semi conductors (soxx) and right now its software (igv). Don't buy igv today, it needs some time to cool off after a 20% run over the last few days.

But as a beginner I'd stick to spy and qqq. You won't make all that much but on the flip side you won't lose much either. A "crash" of spy is 20% and on a 10 year horizon the spy has never been down.

With the war in Iran coming to a close, expect to make 5% on breaking news of a legit deal.

comments.author: UltraMegaTrader
comments.body: Pick one asset and only trade that for a long time. Learn the feel of it.

comments.author: EditorOdd2501
comments.body: Bro, realistically youre currently clueless. Paper trade until you prove to yourself you can be profitable. Hint.. you a long way off

comments.author: Thwerty
comments.body: Don't buy shitty penny stocks just because you heard about them on reddit is 1

comments.author: defstar23
comments.body: Paper trade for a few months first. No reason to lose real money while still learning.

comments.author: MohaveTrader
comments.body:
Not trying to kick you while you're down — but there's a common thread running through all six of these, and it's a learnable one. Every name in here except one would get caught by a basic pre-trade screen. The problem isn't that you picked six unlucky stocks; it's that they're all the *same kind* of stock.

Here's the checklist I run before anything goes in the book. A name has to clear all of these or I don't touch it: I use AI to run it, because it's a lot faster than I am.

**1. Is it on a real exchange (Nasdaq/NYSE), not OTC?**

* **CNTMF (Fluent Corp)** fails instantly — it's an OTCQB sub-penny cannabis name, \~613M shares out, trading around 2–3 cents. OTC pink/QB stocks have the thinnest liquidity and the loosest reporting. A −99% line is what these do. Hard pass before you even look at the chart.

**2. Has it done a recent reverse split?**

* **SPRC (SciSparc)** did a 1-for-9 reverse split on March 4, 2026, dropping shares from \~5M to \~565K — with 2 *billion* shares still authorized. Reverse split + huge authorized share count = a company keeping itself listed while diluting. If you bought before the split, you got run over, which is exactly what a −94% line shows.

**3. Is the company funding itself through constant dilution / is there going-concern language?**

* **OPTT** — going-concern doubt, \~228M shares outstanding, \~$20M cash burn over nine months on \~$7M cash. Funded by convertibles. Sub-$1.
* **LODE (Comstock)** — long history of issuing shares to stay alive.
* **ARBE** — legit radar-chip tech, but a SPAC-era cash-burner that's been diluting and bleeding from over $1.

That's five of your six. They're not bad luck — they're a category: cheap, beaten-down, perpetually-diluting micro-caps. "Cheap and already down a lot" feels safe and is the single most expensive instinct in this corner of the market. These things can always get cheaper, and dilution guarantees the float keeps growing under you.

**The one exception is RXRX (Recursion).** That's an actual company — Nasdaq-listed AI drug-discovery platform, real partnerships, real market cap. No reverse split, no OTC, no predatory financing. Your loss there is just a volatile name held through a downtrend, which is a totally different (and more fixable) problem than the other five. The problem with RXRX its pretty much been on a down trend since March of 2025.

If I could give you one takeaway: **run the screen** ***before*** **you buy, not after you're red.** A 60-second checklist — exchange, reverse-split history, share count trend, going-concern language — would have flagged everything here but RXRX before you put a dollar in. That's the whole game at this end of the market: most of the work is deciding what *not* to touch. 

Good luck digging out. It's recoverable, but step one is not buying the next CNTMF.

replies.author: Argose83
replies.body: Thanks I'm gonna liquidate and probably put in an etf for the time being. Is there a particular AI you suenor will chat gpt be able to answer these??

replies.author: MohaveTrader
replies.body:
I use Claude on desktop, not browser. Here are instructions and I had to have Claude walk me through some of the steps. I do pay for Claude and I even thought you didn't ask for it I pay $25/month for ZENBOT which is a scanner I found that has a lot of nice features.

# Connecting Alpaca Paper Trading to Claude Desktop (MCP)

A step-by-step setup guide for a brand-new user. By the end, Claude Desktop will be able to read your Alpaca paper account and pull live market data in plain language — no coding required during normal use.

**Time required:** \~30–45 minutes the first time **Cost:** Free (paper trading + the MCP server are both free) **Platform:** Written for Windows. Mac steps are noted where they differ.

# What you're building

Alpaca is a brokerage with a free "paper" (simulated) trading account and a full market-data API. The MCP server is a small connector that lets Claude Desktop talk to that account — checking balances, pulling price bars, and (if you choose) placing simulated orders, all by just asking.

There are four stages:

1. Create the Alpaca account and generate API keys
2. Install the two prerequisites (Python and uv)
3. Wire the MCP server into Claude Desktop's config file
4. Verify the connection

# Stage 1 — Alpaca account and API keys

1. **Sign up at alpaca.markets.** When asked which API you want, choose **Trading API** (for trading your own account), *not* Broker API (that's for businesses building their own brokerage).
2. **Make sure you're on the Paper account.** After login there's a toggle between Live and Paper trading. Stay on **Paper** — it comes funded with $100,000 in simulated money and risks nothing real.
3. **Turn on MFA first.** Before it will issue keys, Alpaca prompts you to secure the account. Activate an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, Microsoft Authenticator — any TOTP app). **Save the recovery codes somewhere safe** — losing your phone without them locks you out.
4. **Generate paper API keys.** Click **API** in the left sidebar → generate keys on the paper account. You'll get: Copy both immediately into a password manager. If you ever lose the secret, just regenerate — no real harm, just mildly annoying.
   * A **Key ID** (looks like `PKxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx`)
   * A **Secret Key** (a longer string, shown **only once**)

>

# Stage 2 — Install the prerequisites

You need two things installed once.

1. **Python 3.10 or newer** — from python.org. During install, **check "Add Python to PATH"** (the single most common gotcha).
2. **uv** — a fast package manager that runs the MCP server on demand. Open PowerShell and run:powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm [https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1](https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1) | iex"  *(Mac/Linux:* `curl -LsSf` [`https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh`](https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh) `| sh`*)* Then **close and reopen PowerShell** so `uvx` lands on your PATH.
3. **Verify both installed.** In a fresh PowerShell window:python --version uvx --version  Both should print a version number. If either says "not recognized," it didn't make it onto your PATH — fix that before continuing.

That's the whole prerequisite list. No virtual environments, no repo to clone, no Docker.

# Stage 3 — Wire the MCP server into Claude Desktop

You'll add a small block to one JSON config file.

1. **Open the config file.** The most reliable way, regardless of how Claude Desktop was installed: **Settings → Developer → Edit Config** *(If you prefer the file path directly: standard installs use* `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json` *on Windows, or* `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` *on Mac.* ***Microsoft Store installs are sandboxed*** *and live at a different path — always use the* ***Edit Config*** *button instead of hunting for the file.)*
2. **Paste in the Alpaca server block,** filling in your two keys. Use the **official** server (`alpaca-mcp-server`) — skip third-party forks.{   "mcpServers": {     "alpaca": {       "command": "uvx",       "args": \["alpaca-mcp-server"\],       "env": {         "ALPACA\_API\_KEY": "PKxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx",         "ALPACA\_SECRET\_KEY": "your-secret-key-here",         "ALPACA\_PAPER\_TRADE": "True"       }     }   } }  If the file already has content, merge this `alpaca` entry into the existing `mcpServers` object rather than overwriting it.
3. **Windows PATH gotcha:** Claude Desktop's child processes don't always inherit your interactive PATH, so a bare `"command": "uvx"` can fail to launch. If it does, replace it with the **full path** to uvx, with doubled backslashes:"command": "C:\\\\Users\\\\YOURNAME\\\\.local\\\\bin\\\\uvx.exe", 
4. **Save the file** (Ctrl+S).
5. **Fully restart Claude Desktop.** A normal close often leaves background processes running. On Windows, force-quit in PowerShell:taskkill /F /IM claude.exe /T  *(Mac: quit from the menu bar, or* `killall Claude`*.)* Then relaunch.

# Stage 4 — Verify the connection

1. Go to **Settings → Developer → Local MCP servers.** The `alpaca` server should show **running**. If it shows failed or errored, that panel is where you'll catch it — the usual causes are a typo in a key, a PATH issue with uvx (see Stage 3, step 3), or the file not saving.
2. In a new chat, ask: **"What's my Alpaca balance?"**
3. The first time Claude calls a tool, you'll get a permission prompt. For a read-only call like account info, **Always Allow** is fine. For anything that places orders, keep confirmations on so you approve each one.

A healthy fresh paper account reports roughly: $100,000 equity, $100,000 cash, $200,000 buying power (Reg-T 2x), no open positions, zero day trades.

# Everyday use after setup

Once it's running you never touch the config again. You can just ask things like:

* "What's my Alpaca balance and any open positions?"
* "Pull the daily bars for TSLA over the last 30 days."
* "Show me today's premarket movers." *(depending on which data tools the server exposes)*

# Troubleshooting cheat sheet

|Symptom|Most likely cause|Fix|
|:-|:-|:-|
|Server shows "failed" on launch|uvx not found by Claude's child process|Use the full `uvx.exe` path in `command` (Stage 3, step 3)|
|"not recognized" on `python`/`uvx`|Skipped "Add to PATH"|Reinstall Python with the box checked; reopen PowerShell after installing uv|
|Auth / 403 errors|Wrong or rotated keys|Regenerate keys in Alpaca, paste new values, restart|
|Can't find the config file|Microsoft Store sandbox install|Use Settings → Developer → **Edit Config** instead of the file path|
|Changes not taking effect|App didn't fully restart|`taskkill /F /IM claude.exe /T`, then relaunch|

# Works with other AI tools?

This setup isn't Claude-only. It rides on **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — an open standard, originally from Anthropic, now adopted by OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and others. Any MCP-capable client can connect to the same Alpaca server; only the plumbing differs.

There are two kinds of MCP connection, and which one you use depends on the app:

* **Local (stdio)** — the app launches the server as a process on your own machine. That's exactly what this guide does with `uvx alpaca-mcp-server`. Works in **desktop** apps: Claude Desktop, ChatGPT Desktop, Cursor, VS Code, and similar.
* **Remote (URL)** — the app connects to a server hosted somewhere on the web. **Web** apps (ChatGPT in the browser, Copilot Studio, etc.) can't start a local process, so they need a hosted version of the server and a URL instead of a `command`.

What that means in practice:

|Your AI tool|How to connect Alpaca|
|:-|:-|
|Claude Desktop|This guide as-is (local)|
|ChatGPT Desktop / Cursor / VS Code|Same local pattern — add an equivalent MCP server entry in that app's config|
|ChatGPT (web) / Copilot Studio|Needs a **remote/hosted** Alpaca MCP server and its URL, not the local `uvx` command|

So the capability is portable across the major AI assistants — the account, keys, and Alpaca server stay the same. The only thing that changes is *where the server runs* and how each app's config points at it. Check your specific app's MCP documentation for its exact config format.

*Paper trading only. This guide sets up a simulated account — no real funds are involved. Always keep* `ALPACA_PAPER_TRADE` *set to* `True` *unless you fully understand the implications of live trading.*

comments.author: InterviewEither519
comments.body: Did you do any research before or did you just buy some stuff and hope for the best?

comments.author: After_Government_292
comments.body: Dude get tf out. This is a scam.

comments.author: ninZaco
comments.body:
Brutal honesty from someone who's been there:

**This isn't about instincts** – **this is position sizing and stock selection.**

Every single holding is a low-float, speculative micro/small cap. CNTMF is down 99%. That's not bad luck, that's a pattern.

**What I'd do:**

* Stop buying anything under $5 – seriously
* With \~$961, trade **1 stock max**, learn it deeply
* Switch to **index ETFs** (SPY, QQQ) until you understand why you're entering

AI won't fix this. **Process will.** Your instincts aren't broken – your stock screener is. Start there.
